your values are expressed in what you fund and what you actually prioritize as a company.

know you're chief of staff, and I know you're now in charge of onboarding, yes?

was your underwriting deals to help people with money make more money.

So I guess at a high level, when I first started, one of the first projects Marcel and I worked on was the strategy sprints.

Before we implemented this eight-week accelerated onboarding process, we were just selling a deal based on Marcel's amazing sales skills, but also overselling and putting stress downstream on the delivery team and like what we'd actually execute on, especially as our product and workflows are constantly evolving.
And back then, they were way less powerful than they are now.
We realized that.
And And

And then kind of flipped the model to instead of selling this 12-month contract out of the gate, let's sell a two-month strategy sprint where we actually go in and uncover what the goals are for the stakeholders, start building the relationship early, get all of the materials, information, and access that we need up front.

That's access to systems like Search Console and where we can pull the analytics to actually start tracking the work that we're doing, but also anything the company has in place that can help accelerate our artifact generation and content creation process, which are things like messaging guides, brand guidelines, customer pain point docs, like anything the company already knows about itself, that's super helpful for us to go ingest because ultimately we're creating content for their audience.
The more aligned we can be upfront, the faster our back and forth calibration process with the client will be.
And so a lot of times before that, we were like delivering the first calibration article, hey, how does this look based on all the research that we've done?
And it's just completely off.
And that's like a horrible...
First impression to have with a customer, even though we can get it right quickly.
If that first impression is, wow, this really missed the mark, like what are we doing here?
That leaves a lasting effect on the account.
So for all those reasons combined, the strategy sprint was implemented.
It was just Marcel and I running it with a managing editor, Ada.
And we did that for the first like 10 accounts and then scaled the team up from there.
Once we scaled the team, I became less involved on the six to eight weeks of the process and most involved on the kickoff call, which I can run into.
That's kind of the high level, how it started and then like where I'm at in terms of it now.
And then Andy runs and manages the sprint process as a whole.
Now, I think there's like 15 people on that team doing two new client kickoffs a week.
So that's the sprint.
sprint.

And like that as a pattern, it's more of an anomaly.
Got it.
Okay.
Our overall tracking and measuring and health flagging is definitely an issue organizationally.
Like last week, I just helped with Bridget and I built out a system to help track account health and directors, just director activity, actions, and progress across Notion and Linear.
And so everything a director is doing now is formalized into a ticket.
That ticket is attached to an account and needs to move a specific health metric.
It needs to be a purpose behind the ticket and the size of that.
How much time is this action going to take and what metric is it moving for the account?
And then we prioritize that meeting by accounts that are flagged.
This account's unhealthy.
Why is it unhealthy?
Our content strategy is at a two here.
I need help to do these five things to get it to a five.
Here's the ticket and here's the work to be done and here are the questions that I have.
And now we have we just finished or we just.
It's kicked off the first cycle.
Now that's a whole process of directors have that space to go put their work out, hold themselves accountable to it, and ask for help from the rest of the team as well.
And so that dashboard, once I get back into all the systems, is in Linear?
Yeah, there's two places.
there's the client database by Account Health.

This is what we just built out, and so the directors give their scores every week out of 25, and we run through it, and they also flag the health, so we can see the week-over-week change in health score.
And we can also see it with context of how good of a customer it is for us, and also their MRR.
If this client is a red and it's taking five team members' extra work, is it worth the churn?
Did it just drop this client to be able to deliver an abnormal?
It's also like,
Can I ask you a favor, which I need to ask you, but could you link this to me in Slack so that when I get my systems, I can kind of find it.
I can just go there.
Thank you.
And there's a few views here.
A few views.
So you just said something that's the kind of detail that I'm to want to get under of.
A red flag that five extra team members are working on an account.
What's an example of why five team members have been brought into an account?
That was more of an example and an exaggeration, but.
Okay.
There's like a crystal clear example here.
Like the biggest number that jumps out is Surge.
They're a customer client.
I I can get the backs for how we hire.
We hire them for like work that we don't do.
Really, or that we didn't really do before.
All pretty custom boutique work.
It's taking Jason, who's one of our best, well, head of go-to-market, but one of our best directors as well, the head of account managers.
It's taking them a bunch of time, and it requires two to three other full-time people under it.
For two months, it was just me and someone else.
This is something that, from the beginning, should have been flagged as, let's throw 5x the number of people at it, because it's worth 5x.
To my exaggerated example.
More so, prioritizing capacity based on contract value.
But honestly, there's more than just contract value.
This, yes, we should probably keep this MRR.
It's amazing.
But also, the type of work that we're delivering.
Pre-ICP, we were selling a lot of, like, out-of-scope custom work and, like, difficult work with clients.
And so, is that as worth it?
That's taking a little more of the team's time versus bread and butter client that came in that loves us, that gives us control that we haven't done before.
Like those are also conversations that only now we can start having because we have like the scoring in place and things like that.
So it's still pretty new.
Okay.
Yeah, that's one of the things that just heads up that I have like on my writer of how can I support all of you of ongoing segmentation.
And like, let's say it's anyone under 10K MR, you know, do they get a different type of like set it up in the contract, set it up in the kickoff expectations.
They get a different level type of support versus, you know, resource allocation and whatnot.
But being more intentional about it, my impression based on what everybody else has said is up until now, it's been everybody gets the same.
Is that fair?
Pretty much.
Outside of escalations when all of a sudden it's like all hands on deck because it's become an escalation situation.
Yeah, outside of like super custom clients, like Webflow, Jason's on, Augment Code, Jason's on, this is all changing 12.
One with the new re-organic structure, I'm on, Kyle is on one or two accounts.
so those are like more custom-revolved accounts.
Same sound.
Okay.

Well, if you could tell me like right now with the growth and what you're doing, you know, again, my impression is that sort of the seasoned leaders who really know the domain, really know marketing, really know AI, are more AI native.
Again, sort of all roads lead to a few, a few that need to be in there.
Can you tell me right now what you consider the team's biggest pain points with growth and customer engagement?
And then sort of if you had a magic wand to ask me, Ella, go focus these things.
What would those, what's the pain and what would you ask of me?
It's a good question.
I think there's two things.
things.
That I feel the most in terms of pain that are clear, maybe three.

The first is like context sharing across the company, even between the leaders themselves, because I have to jump into so many things that work with Marcel and like most things I do are new and I haven't done before.
I'm getting better at it and I think I'm getting a lot of the context, but I'm still nowhere close to like being able to put it all together and communicate it like Marcel is.
And each leader is pretty siloed in what they're doing at a time where like we're at this point where we're working on two different products.
They're going to be released and it's going to change the way the business looks and operates pretty soon.
That's at least the vision.
Even this delivery model that we're talking about, like the goal isn't to scale to 100 clients under this model.
goal.
Right.
Exactly.
Right.
And so and then to do that, you need like all the pipelines and the workflows working and you need to understand all the pain points within the delivery org.
And so I guess the.
The of it all and the sharing of context and like when a new efficiency is found in engineering that really helps with a specific workflow, right?
We don't share that to the delivery team that's actually doing the work as if I could.
Like I've been on calls where I saw one thing in a meeting from Marcel and Daniel because I asked or I was doing something with them.
In another meeting, an account strategist, after I asked, and again, this might be the root is like people don't speak up enough.
Like I had to ask, what's taking so much time?
Why do you look tired?
Like you literally look tired.
Why do you look tired?
Why do you get up to sleep?
Oh, I was working on setting up Scrunch, which is an AI visibility tool for a bunch of our clients.
Oh, how much time is that taking you?
How are you doing it?
When I understood kind of like the why behind it, it was just taking them a long time to come up with the prompts and then put them in.
And Daniel, the day prior, showed me this workflow that he built for our AI visibility tool that we launched as a company that most people don't even realize.
Prompts that he used to make that work, or workflows that he used to make that work, our team didn't even know about.
And so I showed Eric on the call where to find the workflow, how to operate it, all of that.
And that's a simple thing.
Hey, we literally launched this AI visibility tool.
These are the workflows we built to help improve prompts and find them and organize them in buckets and make them specific to customers and their personas.
Like, I know you all are already doing that work.
Here's where you can go to kind of build efficiency.

How do you, George, how do you today as a company share, like, do you guys have company standup, like dev team standups, like where, if you're going to have Daniel or the team come in and give like the top three innovations they've made that'll make the team's life more easy that everybody gets at the same time?
Is there already a meeting, already a forum where that can be delivered and it just needs to be intentional?
We do.
do.
All hands twice or by monthly, but it's more so highlighting wins in major progress versus in the weeds type of things.

And just recently we launched workflow checks where a Panzer or a Marcel would jump in and actually ask the directors or the editors what is taking the most time and where they'll show them the process that they run.
And so, and alongside that, Panda is building out a ton of documentation to help step by step, but again, definitely an area for improvement.

I think the community will help if we organize that and make it really good and make like, here's your onboarding plan for growthx to how to be successful as a managing editor, as a director, as whatever it is.
Because it's really on the person to go get really good really quickly.
Yeah.
Yeah, that's been pretty fun.

So my experience as being onboarded is that I was given access to the tools and then that was it, you know, was go find, you know, so I felt kind of like a jerk at first because like, hey, you know, Bridget, can you point me to all the onboarding stuff that people are talking about in Slack?
But then I finally got into Notion.
like, oh, nevermind.
I found it.
found all the other, you know, type of thing.
But it really is, at least it seems incumbent that people come in and then they just have to go consume and then proactively ask questions about what they don't understand.
Is that fair?
Yeah, I think I might be a more unique case because I don't fall into like a track of role in the company.
So there might be a better onboarding for other people.
I'm assuming now it's better than when I started, but I'm just not as plugged into what that looks like.
Okay.
Okay.
I kind of considered it maybe a positive one because the content is so amazing at your company internally.
So, and I think it's.
It's almost like a personality type you need to thrive at GrowthX as someone who can go figure all that out.
But some of my impression, my hypothesis is that an operating model of how are you with all that content?
How are you as a team making sure broadly you're sharing Intel across all, raising all boats with knowledge, having more consistent interaction with customers?
So there's like an operating model on top of all this great stuff that, which you're referring to in the operating model that got you to 55 customers shouldn't be the one that gets you to 100.
It'll break.
Okay.
And there's probably a lot to do, like, has done a lot of documentation work for the delivery side of the org.
I missed what that was.
Panzerino is his name?
Yes.
Yeah, yeah.
I'm meeting with him later.
Yeah.
Nice.
Yeah.
That'll be great.
So you said two pains.
One was context sharing across the company.
What was your second?
You said maybe three.
Okay.
Second was goals and focus and starting top down with leadership.

If it isn't Marcel and Bridget's good at it now and I'm learning and it's a big focus of mine to get better at it, but the leadership meetings we run up until two weeks ago when we kind of changed everything are not goal or metric.
We're work stream driven and the leaders aren't doing much besides just reporting every week on what they've done to make that meeting effective.

And so it's on me to process, I don't care about the work, it's really important, like to process their weekly updates and then align them with the work streams that we set out to do as a company and then pull in numbers and goals to align there.
so like the whole prioritization structure, I think should start with the leaders themselves.
And honestly, it's on Marcel, Marcel.
Uh, and us to, to help the leaders do that, but being hyper-focused on the goals we're driving versus just the actions we're taking or the activity that we're doing.
Like, I mean, I'm reading the reports of what the leader, the leaders do and, um, some are good, but I have to turn it into like problem solution.
Where's your answer?
Like, don't just tell me you had three, um, listening to our meetings with clients.
Tell me what clients, how they went, how many do you have left of clients that are renewing that you still need to do?
Are any flags?
Like, that's the way we should be prioritizing across all the, the parts of the business.
And so we're working on it, but I think it's definitely an area that we can build leverage in.
Yeah.
Okay, cool.
That makes total sense.
Um, as I get into this, anything that you would ask of me, if you're like, Hey, Ella, if we're going to drop you in here, uh, Uh, my.
My life would be better if you focused on X or you solved X.
Yeah, solve the focus.
I can probably get back to you on that one, but I think delivery is clearly the focus right now and it's pain for everyone.
And so that whole system from Strategy Sprint through the eight weeks and then passed the handoff to actually like a stable account, super messy.
We're going through a reorg starting 12-1.
I've been less involved in it, so I just don't know how it's going to play out, but I just know progress needs to be made there.

And again, right now it's all in Ocean and like it's me who stitched together these things with Bridget and like we're pulling it manually right now with my EA and like it's, there's a lot more we can do here to run it like a machine, to run it like a machine.
Okay.
I didn't know this was manual, manual pulled.
Well, it kind of needs to be manual right now because leaders go in and they update each week on the score and the why, behind why change.
And so then these just get pulled down to the table and then automatically updated.
I I see.
It's not a mistake.
And then everything is, again, tied to, I'm pulling up linear right now, just to show you quick, and I can link it to you as well.
So my other screen is linked to the director's print.
Now, this, this didn't exist two weeks ago.
Now, everyone who has a ticket associated with it and accounts associated with it, what size is the bat, this is an example of bad ticket.
Let's see, one that was done here.
This is the context.
Here's what we're doing.
How I'm going to approach this, what I'm going to deliver by the end of this two-week cycle.
So it's way more focused.
How do we want to move?
Here's the size of bed.
Here's the director responsible, et cetera.
So the idea was now that we can score each client here, we see the health score, we can flag that stay on top of the work actually being done with the help of our directors.
And George, random question.
I think we're over, so I need to let you go.
But my last question is, just you and your role, like again, like if you weren't pulled into things because you needed to be, what is your, like ideally, what would your day-to-day role be?

Yeah, I mean, I chose it to be generalist because it's still something that I'm figuring out, but I like, I don't like the repetitive, let's meet every day or let's meet weekly, like the repetitive nature of some systems and processes that like, I like staying on top of things, what's the thing the future?
That doesn't excite me.
So day-to-day, it wouldn't be like managing the strategy sprints end-to-end and managing just the people and the work being done.
I like going and fixing and doing.
And I like selling and learning.
my ideal day would be like working on the fixing the kickoffs and the pre-sprint process so we can actually turn it into like a sellable program where we set up your platform.
Here are the things we're handing off to you.
Now go use the platform yourself.
And that's like the only scalable way to actually skyrocket this business.
So that's super exciting.
And it's work that I've already done manually.
like it's coming in January.
It'll be a focus.
But things like that, those special projects that really drive things forward are exciting.
Helping everybody, including myself and the leader, stay focused on the goals and like coming up with those goals, staying on top of them, working with Marcel and Brady.
Like, that's exciting to me as well.
And yeah, I just like helping and like working on the hardest stuff.
And so I think those two projects are like big examples of what, you know.
Okay.

And right now, things pulling you away from that are like the fire drill around, hey, we need some extra help in the strategy kickoffs or take over some customer accounts or like those are the things that are pulling you.
Is that fair?
Yeah, kind of real quickly, like where I spend my time.
Now, right now, Lovables is a super custom account that we can't give to anyone else.
That takes a bunch of my time.
That takes 10% of my time.
This special project, which was cool, like the system I just showed you for directors and the client database stood that up in like a week and a half.
So that took some time, the leadership meeting stuff and staying on top of the leaders, that took the rest.
And then like some typical chief of staff stuff.
There's like two EAs that I have to manage every day.
Obviously, there's people asking for Marcel.
Marcel.
Marcel.
I have to prioritize.
Is that actually important?
Can I fix it?
Who do need?
Does it go to ourself, et cetera?
Okay, super.
All right, awesome.
All right.
Well, I want to be respectful of your time, so let me let you go.
But I'm sure I will be back.

So just so you know what my process is, is I'm interviewing the leadership kind of team right now, and I'm pulling together generally people's observations of their roles, what's working in the delivery team, when customers are taking the most space, et cetera, and kind of forming some hypotheses.
And then Bridget will introduce me sort of down to the folks working like in the pods, et cetera, to kind of triage all of that.

And then my hope is to help support pretty quickly, like what can, you're doing this reorg, but as you're preparing for February and these product launches, what is kind of the operating model and what do you need to hire or what do you need to offload in different ways to get to that, you know, 50 million and beyond that you want to get to.
And then the marketing side as well, like we've got, we have a lot of work that we need to tee up and set up there, like in terms of launch, product launch.
I'm like, yeah.
Yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah.
And all that, so.
Yeah, cool.
All right, well, thank you so much for your time and for your patience while I was locked out of all my tools.
I appreciate it.
I'm assuming I will be able to access, will I be able to access Fireflies, like the note taker?
And if not, would you mind sharing?
Yeah, yeah, yeah, for sure.
I'll share this one with you.
Awesome.
And hit me up for anything you need in terms of onboarding or, hey, do we have this resource?
Like, I'm happy to help.
Super.
All right, take care.
Have a great rest of your day.
You too, bye.
Bye.

default_summary.markdown_formatted:
## Meeting Purpose

[Ella's discovery call with George on GrowthX's operational challenges.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=555.0)

## Key Takeaways

  - [**Strategy Sprints are a success but have operational gaps.** The 8-week sprints de-risk engagements by securing upfront alignment and access, but their execution lacks rigor, causing churn (e.g., LightSource) and failed conversions (e.g., Bubble).](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=1658.0)
  - [**Two core pains hinder scalability:** 1) Siloed knowledge prevents delivery teams from using engineering's efficiency tools (e.g., AI visibility workflows). 2) Leadership meetings are activity-based reports, not goal-driven strategy sessions.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=2314.0)
  - [**A new manual system tracks account health,** but its manual nature and lack of integration with the sprint process limit its effectiveness in proactively flagging risks.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=1982.0)
  - [**George's time is split between fire-fighting and strategic projects.** His ideal role is focused on scalable initiatives, but he's pulled into operational gaps like running Strategy Sprint kickoffs and managing custom accounts.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=2970.0)

## Topics

### The Problem: Scaling Pains

  - [**Context:** GrowthX's rapid growth to 55 delivery staff has outpaced its operational model, creating bottlenecks and inefficiencies.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=555.0)
  - [**Ella's Role:** Build a scalable operating model for the full customer journey (sales → delivery → retention) to support the upcoming product launches and the goal of scaling beyond 100 clients.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=3133.0)

### The Solution: Strategy Sprints

  - [**Purpose:** A mandatory 8-week sprint to de-risk engagements and improve downstream execution by securing upfront alignment and access.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=807.0)
  - [**Process:**](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=991.0)
      - [**ICP Matrix:** A scoreable matrix qualifies clients on factors like trust, long-term buy-in, and a streamlined approval process.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=991.0)
      - [**Sales Handoff:** A detailed doc transfers context from sales to the sprint team.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=1343.0)
      - [**Kickoff Call:** A 1-hour deep dive with key stakeholders to define goals and identify knowledge gaps.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=1387.0)
  - [**Ownership:** Andy and Ada now manage the sprint process, which includes daily standups and Linear tracking.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=1826.0)

### The Gaps: Where Sprints Fail

  - [**Execution Failures:**](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=1658.0)
      - [**LightSource (Churned):** Churned due to a content backlog stuck in legal review, blocking publication and preventing signals of success.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=1658.0)
      - [**Bubble (Failed Conversion):** Failed to convert after the sprint because a promised technical SEO audit was not delivered.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=1732.0)
  - [**Root Cause:** The sprint process lacks operational rigor to track specific SoW milestones and proactively flag risks.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=1826.0)

### The New System: Account Health Tracking

  - [**Purpose:** A new system to track account health and director activity, built by George and Bridget.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=1982.0)
  - [**Components:**](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=1982.0)
      - [**Notion Dashboard:** Directors score accounts weekly (out of 25) and flag health status.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=2052.0)
      - [**Linear Tickets:** All director work is formalized into tickets tied to accounts and specific health metrics.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=1990.0)
  - [**Limitation:** The system is manual and not integrated with the Strategy Sprint process, limiting its ability to proactively flag issues early in the engagement.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=1982.0)

### The Pain Points: Systemic Issues

  - [**1. Context Sharing:** Siloed knowledge prevents delivery teams from using efficiency tools built by engineering.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=2343.0)
      - [**Example:** Delivery teams were manually creating AI visibility prompts, unaware of a pre-built workflow from Daniel (CTO) that could automate the task.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=2422.0)
  - [**2. Leadership Focus:** Leadership meetings are activity-based reports, not goal-driven strategy sessions.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=2701.0)
      - [**Impact:** This creates a top-down prioritization issue that George must manually correct.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=2701.0)

### George's Ideal Role

  - [**Preference:** Strategic, project-based work (e.g., fixing kickoffs, developing a scalable platform model) over repetitive process management.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=2970.0)
  - [**Current Time Allocation:**](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=3074.0)
      - [**10%:** Managing the custom Lovable account.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=3087.0)
      - [**\~50%:** Strategic projects (e.g., building the account health system).](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=3087.0)
      - [**\~40%:** CoS duties (e.g., managing EAs, prioritizing Marcel's requests).](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=3087.0)

## Next Steps

  - [**Ella:**](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=3133.0)
      - [Continue interviewing leadership to form hypotheses on operational gaps.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=3133.0)
      - [Get system access (Notion, Linear) to review the new account health dashboard.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=2052.0)
      - [Partner with Bridget to interview delivery team members.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=3133.0)
      - [Develop a scalable operating model to support the upcoming product launches.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=3133.0)
  - [**George:**](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=2101.0)
      - [Share links to the account health dashboard and director sprint board.](https://fathom.video/share/wGC-b9cfmJoi5mqNyu_f_FYZrGAyy3ez?tab=summary&timestamp=2101.0)

action_items.description: Send Ella Slack links to kickoff agenda, ICP matrix, client health dashboard, Linear directors' sprint, sample kickoff recordings
